text:
This monograph is a new attempt at the evaluation of the Polish-Moldavian relations during the great period of transition from the Middle Ages to modern times (1492–1538). Where previous literature focused mainly on individual wars and battles, this study takes diplomacy into the context of a broad geopolitical structure of the times, analyzing the interactions of the Kingdom of Poland and the Principality of Moldavia within the framework of the political system of the Kingdom of Hungary and the Ottoman Empire.

The work discusses the Polish policy towards Moldavia during the reigns of the Jagiellons’ successors: John Albert, Alexander, and Sigismund I the Old. It is focused on: - the legal and political status of Moldavia in the time of Stephen the Great, - the role of humanism and the idea of Antemurale Christianitatis in Jagiellonian diplomacy; - the great magnates’ role in the conflict at the border of the Kingdom of Poland: for example, the Chodeckis. The essential theme of the work is the controversy over the Pokuttya, and the consequences of the victory at Mohács and the ascension to the throne of Petru Rareș.

Based on rich source material from Poland, Romania, Austria, Hungary and Germany, Grounds of Contest draws on hitherto unused diplomatic correspondence and treaties to reexamine the political interaction in the region from a new perspective. Moving beyond the traditional Polonocentric perspective, the study highlights Moldavia as a focal point of diplomatic struggle between the Jagiellonians, the Habsburgs and the Sublime Porte. The book concludes with 1538, the year in which Ottoman suzerainty over Moldavia was established for good and the conditions for the new configuration of the region were met.
